next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

The Pike Foundation Hall had been built to convince people that history could be polished. The old warehouse beams still ran black above the chandeliers, but every bolt had been sanded and lacquered and lit from below. White linen covered the tables in strict rows, and brass lamps made the place glow like a courtroom trying to pass for a church. A jazz trio stood near the staircase and played too softly to offend anyone with money.

Lena stood at the back wall with one hand on her radio and watched donors smile through their fear. The panic lived in small things. Men who kept touching their phones under the tablecloth. Women who laughed and kept their wineglass by the stem so their hands would not shake the bowl. Two bankers in blue suits argued over whispers and never stopped looking toward the loading dock doors where the protest noise leaked in each time a server crossed through. Everyone in the room was pretending this was another gala year, but nobody had come to celebrate.

Her right knee scar ached before storms and before bad scenes. Tonight it had started in the parking lot.

Beau came up on her left, broad shoulders filling the aisle, his right wrist strapped in a black brace that looked too tight. He had wrapped the brace with gray duct tape at the edge where it rubbed his skin raw. He held his flashlight in his left hand because the right still did not trust him, and he kept flexing his fingers like he could argue the pain down if he moved enough.

"Contractor stacked floral crates in the service cut-through again," he said. "Camera two's blind, ju...
open_hooks_to_carry: Preparation chapter sustains dread without immediate payoff.
Pike privately concedes she benefited from the tract sale.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...evee and rattled the annex glass in its frame.

At 2:03 a.m., she called the sheriff's duty line and logged a threat assessment tied to Thomas Pike's trust authority and forthcoming disbursement schedule. She kept her voice measured, gave no flourish, asked for immediate protective planning conference at first light. The deputy taking the call sounded nineteen and terrified of spelling anything wrong.

When she hung up, she took the hot-sauce packet Ida had pressed into her hand and tore it with her teeth, squeezing a stripe onto a stale pecan roll. Mara laughed once, startled and tired and real.

"That is revolting," Mara said.

"It's calories," Lena said, chewing.

"It's a hate crime against pastry."

"Put it in your notes."

Mara did, because of course she did.

Lena taped the yellow pad page to the wall above the contact sheets, drew a box around 49 HOURS, and underlined it until the pen tore paper.

The case they could prove in court was still broken open on the floor somewhere between county pride and federal jurisdiction. The case they could act on was suddenly plain: a man with a signature, a schedule everyone knew, and a county that kept pretending violence arrived like weather instead of paperwork.

She looked once at the serial number on the glove wrapper before sliding it into an evidence sleeve marked OFF-RECORD LEAD. Then she set a new legal pad on top of the old one and started building the protection grid for Thomas Pike's house, courthouse route, and gala ingress, line by line, while the storm leaned harder against Mercer County and morning crept in anyway.
state_deltas_required: Prevent an anticipated attack at the Pike Foundation gala with inadequate authority.
Lena commits to a perimeter plan she knows can fail, accepting foreseeable risk.
